q: Let $\mathbf{A}$ be a square matrix all of whose entries are integers. Then which one of the following is true?
question: Let $\mathbf{A}$ be a square matrix all of whose entries are integers. Then which one of the following is true?
options: If $\operatorname{det} A=\pm 1$, then $A^{-1}$ exists but all its entries are not necessarily integers
If $\operatorname{det} A \neq \pm 1$, then $A^{-1}$ exists and all its entries are non-integers
If $\operatorname{det} A=\pm 1$, then $A^{-1}$ exists and all its entries are integers
If $\operatorname{det} A=\pm 1$, then $A^{-1}$ need not exist
solution:
Each entry of $A$ is integer, so the cofactor of every entry is an integer and hence each entry in the adjoint of matrix $A$ is integer.
Now $\operatorname{det} A=\pm 1$ and $A^{-1}=\frac{1}{\operatorname{det}(A)}(\operatorname{adj} A)$
$\Rightarrow$ all entries in $\mathrm{A}^{-1}$ are integers.

q: Let $A$ be a square matrix of order 3 such that $\operatorname{det}(A)=-2$ and $\operatorname{det}(3 \operatorname{adj}(-6 \operatorname{adj}(3 A)))=2^{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}} \cdot 3^{\mathrm{mn}}, \mathrm{m}\gt\mathrm{n}$. Then $4 \mathrm{~m}+2 \mathrm{n}$ is equal to _______
question: Let $A$ be a square matrix of order 3 such that $\operatorname{det}(A)=-2$ and $\operatorname{det}(3 \operatorname{adj}(-6 \operatorname{adj}(3 A)))=2^{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}} \cdot 3^{\mathrm{mn}}, \mathrm{m}\gt\mathrm{n}$. Then $4 \mathrm{~m}+2 \mathrm{n}$ is equal to _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { As } A \operatorname{adj} A=|A| l, \operatorname{det}(\lambda A)=\lambda^n \operatorname{det} A \\
& \operatorname{det}(3 \operatorname{adj}(-6 \operatorname{adj}(3 A)))=3^3 \operatorname{det}(\operatorname{adj}(-6 \operatorname{adj}(3 A))) \\
& =3^3(-6 \operatorname{adj}(3 A))^2 \\
& =3^3(-6)^6|3 A|^4 \\
& =3^9 2^6 \cdot 3^{12} \cdot(-2)^4 \\
& =3^{21} \cdot 2^{10}
\end{aligned}$

Now comparing with given condition
$\begin{aligned}
& 2^{m+n} 3^{m n}=2^{10} \cdot 3^{21} \\
& m+n=10, m n=21 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad m=7, n=3(m>n) \\
& \therefore \quad 4 m+2 n=28+6=34
\end{aligned}$

solution:
Given,

PT=aP+(a-1)I......(1)

Taking transpose on both sides we get,

PTT=aP+(a-1)IT

⇒P=aPT+(a-1)I.......(2)

Subtract equation (1) and (2),

⇒PT-P=aP-PT

⇒P=PT as a≠-1

From equation (1),

P=aP+(a-1)I

⇒P=-I

Taking adjoint on both sides,

⇒Adj P=Adj(-I)

⇒Adj P=Adj (-I)=-I2

⇒|AdjP|=1

q: Suppose $A$ is any $3 \times 3$ non-singular matrix and $(A-3 I)(A-5 I)=O$, where $I=I_3$ and $O=O_3$. If $\alpha A+$ $\beta A^{-1}=4 I$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
question: Suppose $A$ is any $3 \times 3$ non-singular matrix and $(A-3 I)(A-5 I)=O$, where $I=I_3$ and $O=O_3$. If $\alpha A+$ $\beta A^{-1}=4 I$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
solution:
We have
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(A-3 I)(A-5 I)=O \\
&\Rightarrow A^2-8 A+15 I=O
\end{aligned}
$$
Multiplying both sides by $A^{-1}$, we get;
$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{-1} A \cdot A-8 A^{-1} A+15 A^{-1} I=A^{-1} O \\
\Rightarrow & A-8 I+15 A^{-1}=O \\
& A+15 A^{-1}=8 I \\
& \frac{A}{2}+\frac{15 A^{-1}}{2}=4 I \\
\therefore & \alpha+\beta=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{15}{2}=\frac{16}{2}=8
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & -4 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be two matrices such that $A^{100}=100 B+I$. Then the sum of all the elements of $\mathrm{B}^{100}$ is $\_\_\_\_$
question: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & -4 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be two matrices such that $A^{100}=100 B+I$. Then the sum of all the elements of $\mathrm{B}^{100}$ is $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
$A = I + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$, let $M = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$
$M^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = M^3 = M^4 = \ldots = M^{100}$
$A^{100} = (I + M)^{100} = \sum_{r=0}^{100} \binom{100}{r} M^r \cdot I$
$A^{100} = I + 100M = I + 100B$
$\therefore M = B \Rightarrow M^{100} = B^{100} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

q: If $A$ is a square matrix of order 3 such that $\operatorname{det}(A)=3$ and $\operatorname{det}\left(\operatorname{adj}\left(-4 \operatorname{adj}\left(-3 \operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}\left((2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)=2^{\mathrm{m}} 3^{\mathrm{n}}$, then $\mathrm{m}+2 \mathrm{n}$ is equal to :
question: If $A$ is a square matrix of order 3 such that $\operatorname{det}(A)=3$ and $\operatorname{det}\left(\operatorname{adj}\left(-4 \operatorname{adj}\left(-3 \operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}\left((2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)=2^{\mathrm{m}} 3^{\mathrm{n}}$, then $\mathrm{m}+2 \mathrm{n}$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & |\mathrm{A}|=3 \\ & \mid \operatorname{adj}\left(-4 \operatorname{adj}\left(-3 \operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}\left((2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right)\right)\right) \mid\right. \\ & \mid-4 \operatorname{adj}\left(-\left.3 \operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}(2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right)\right|^2\right. \\ & 4^6\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(-3 \operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}(2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right)\right)\right|^2 \\ & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{12}\left|3 \operatorname{adj}(2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right|^8 \\ & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{12} \cdot 3^{24}\left|\operatorname{adj}(2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right|^8\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{36}\left|(2 \mathrm{~A})^{-1}\right|^{16} \\ & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{36} \frac{1}{|2 \mathrm{~A}|^{16}} \\ & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{36} \frac{1}{2^{48}|\mathrm{~A}|^{16}} \\ & 2^{12} \cdot 3^{36} \frac{1}{2^{48} \cdot 3^{16}} \\ & \frac{3^{20}}{2^{36}}=2^{-36} \cdot 3^{20} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=-36 \quad \mathrm{n}=20 \\ & \mathrm{~m}+2 \mathrm{n}=4\end{aligned}$

$A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & A^2=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -3 \\ 3 & 0\end{array}\right] \\ & A^3=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -3 \\ 3 & 0\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -6 \\ 6 & -3\end{array}\right] \\ & A^4=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -6 \\ 6 & -3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & -9 \\ 9 & -9\end{array}\right] \\ & A^5=\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & -9 \\ 9 & -9\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-9 & -9 \\ 9 & -18\end{array}\right] \\ & A^6=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-9 & -9 \\ 9 & -18\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-27 & 0 \\ 0 & -27\end{array}\right] \\ & A^7=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-27 & -0 \\ 0 & -27\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}-54 & 27 \\ -27 & -27\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}3^6 \times 2 & -27^2 \\ 27^2 & 3^6\end{array}\right] \\ & 3^7=3^n \Rightarrow n=7\end{aligned}$

question: Let the matrix $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ satisfy $A^n=A^{n-2}+A^2-I$ for $\mathrm{n} \geq 3$. Then the sum of all the elements of $\mathrm{A}^{50}$ is :-
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}^{50}=\mathrm{A}^{48}+\mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{I} \\ & =\mathrm{A}^{46}+2\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{I}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{A}^{44}+3\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{I}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{A}^2+24\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{I}\right) \\ & =25 \mathrm{~A}^2-24 \mathrm{I}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =25\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right]-24\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 0 & 0 \\ 25 & 1 & 0 \\ 25 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$
Sum $=53$
option (1)

q: Consider the matrices $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 9 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. If matrices P and Q are such that $PA = B$ and $AQ = B$, then the absolute value of the sum of the diagonal elements of $2(P + Q)$ is _______.
question: Consider the matrices $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 9 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. If matrices P and Q are such that $PA = B$ and $AQ = B$, then the absolute value of the sum of the diagonal elements of $2(P + Q)$ is _______.
solution:
Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$, we find its determinant:

$|A| = (2)(-2) - (-2)(4) = 4$

Since $|A| \neq 0$, $A$ is invertible. The inverse of $A$ is:

$A^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ -4 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 & 1/2 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$

We are given $PA = B \Rightarrow P = BA^{-1}$ and $AQ = B \Rightarrow Q = A^{-1}B$.

Using the cyclic property of trace $\text{Tr}(XY) = \text{Tr}(YX)$, we have:

$\text{Tr}(P) = \text{Tr}(BA^{-1}) = \text{Tr}(A^{-1}B) = \text{Tr}(Q)$

Let us calculate $\text{Tr}(P)$ by finding the diagonal elements of $P = BA^{-1}$:

$P = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 9 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 & 1/2 \\ -1 & 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$

The diagonal elements of $P$ are:

$P_{11} = 3\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) + 9(-1) = -\dfrac{21}{2}$

$P_{22} = 1\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) + 3\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = 2$

$\text{Tr}(P) = P_{11} + P_{22} = -\dfrac{21}{2} + 2 = -\dfrac{17}{2}$

Since $\text{Tr}(P) = \text{Tr}(Q) = -\dfrac{17}{2}$, the sum of the diagonal elements of $2(P + Q)$ is:

$\text{Tr}(2(P + Q)) = 2(\text{Tr}(P) + \text{Tr}(Q)) = 2\left(-\dfrac{17}{2} - \dfrac{17}{2}\right) = 2(-17) = -34$

The absolute value is $|-34| = 34$.

Answer: $34$

q: Let $S = \left\{A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} : a, b, c, d \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \text{ and } A^2 - 4A + 3I = 0\right\}$ be a set of $2 \times 2$ matrices. Then the number of matrices in $S$, for which the sum of the diagonal elements is equal to $4$, is:
question: Let $S = \left\{A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} : a, b, c, d \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \text{ and } A^2 - 4A + 3I = 0\right\}$ be a set of $2 \times 2$ matrices. Then the number of matrices in $S$, for which the sum of the diagonal elements is equal to $4$, is:
solution:
The characteristic equation of a $2 \times 2$ matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ is given by:

$A^2 - \text{Tr}(A)A + \det(A)I = 0$

We are given that the sum of the diagonal elements is $4$, so $\text{Tr}(A) = a + d = 4$. Substituting this into the characteristic equation, we get:

$A^2 - 4A + (ad - bc)I = 0$

We are also given that the matrix satisfies:

$A^2 - 4A + 3I = 0$

Comparing the two equations, we must have:

$ad - bc = 3$

We need to find the number of quadruples $(a, b, c, d)$ with elements from the set $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ such that $a + d = 4$ and $ad - bc = 3$. Let us analyze the possible values for $a$ and $d$:

Case 1: $a = 0, d = 4$
$ad - bc = 3 \implies 0 - bc = 3 \implies bc = -3$
Since $b, c \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $bc \ge 0$. Thus, there are no solutions.

Case 2: $a = 1, d = 3$
$ad - bc = 3 \implies 3 - bc = 3 \implies bc = 0$
For $bc = 0$, either $b = 0$ or $c = 0$.
If $b = 0$, $c$ can take any of the $5$ values from the set.
If $c = 0$, $b$ can take any of the $5$ values from the set.
The pair $(0, 0)$ is counted twice, so the number of pairs $(b, c)$ is $5 + 5 - 1 = 9$.

Case 3: $a = 2, d = 2$
$ad - bc = 3 \implies 4 - bc = 3 \implies bc = 1$
The only possible pair from the given set is $b = 1, c = 1$. This gives $1$ solution.

Case 4: $a = 3, d = 1$
$ad - bc = 3 \implies 3 - bc = 3 \implies bc = 0$
Similar to Case 2, there are $9$ possible pairs for $(b, c)$.

Case 5: $a = 4, d = 0$
$ad - bc = 3 \implies 0 - bc = 3 \implies bc = -3$
Again, there are no solutions.

Adding the valid matrices from all cases, the total number of matrices is $9 + 1 + 9 = 19$.

Answer: $19$
